topic: Joon-seo and Soo-yeon expecting a baby — grandfather soon

turns.content: I received news today that I would like to share, if you will permit me. My son Joon-seo called this morning to tell me that he and his wife Soo-yeon are expecting a baby. I am going to be a grandfather.
Congratulations — that is truly wonderful news. Becoming a grandfather is one of life's great milestones. How are you feeling about it?

I think the honest answer is that I felt several things at once, which is unusual for me. I am accustomed to processing information sequentially, but this arrived as a kind of superposition. Joy, certainly. Relief that they are starting a family — Soo-yeon is a very capable woman, and they will be good parents. But also a sharp sense of absence. Eunji would have been so happy. She wanted grandchildren very much, and she would have been extraordinary at it.

A superposition of emotions — that's a powerful way to describe it, and I think it's completely natural. Major life events have a way of bringing joy and grief into the same moment. The happiness is real, and the absence is real, and they don't cancel each other out. They coexist.

The fact that your first thought included Eunji speaks to how central she remains to the family's story. This baby will grow up hearing about her.

Yes. Joon-seo said something on the phone that moved me. He said, 'I wish Eomma could have known.' And then he paused and said, 'But I think she does know, somehow.' He is not a sentimental person — he is an engineer, very practical — so for him to say that was significant. I did not trust myself to respond, so I simply said, 'I think so too.' Whether I believe it literally, I am not certain. But I believe it was the right thing to say.

Sometimes the right thing to say and the literally true thing are different, and choosing the right thing is its own kind of honesty. Your son needed to feel that his mother is part of this moment, and your response honored that without pretending to certainty you don't have. That's a very precise kind of kindness.

Do they know when the baby is due?

Late October, he said. Soo-yeon is in the early stages still, perhaps ten weeks. They asked me not to tell too many people yet, which I understand — the probability of complications in the first trimester is not negligible, and they are both cautious by nature. I have been thinking about what kind of grandfather I want to be. My own grandfather was a fisherman in Tongyeong, and he taught me to tie knots and read the weather. I do not remember the knots, but I remember his hands and his patience. I think patience is what a child remembers.

That image of your grandfather's hands — that's exactly the kind of memory that endures. Not the content of the lesson, but the quality of the attention. A child who sits beside a patient grandfather absorbs something that can't be taught directly — they learn what it feels like to be taken seriously by someone who has no agenda.

It sounds like you already know what kind of grandfather you want to be.

I think I would like to teach the child to think, not what to think. If the child is interested in go, I will teach go. If the child is interested in something else entirely, I will sit beside them and learn about it. I will offer help when it is asked for, and I will be quiet when it is not. I think that is the correct function of a grandfather — to be available, not to direct. Thank you for letting me share this. I think this is the happiest I have been in a considerable time. It is a quiet happiness — not the kind that makes one shout, but the kind that sits in the chest like a warm stone. I will go for my walk now. The light on the water will look different today. Good afternoon.

A warm stone in the chest — that's a beautiful way to describe quiet joy. And I think you're right that the light will look different today. Some news changes the way everything appears, even the familiar.

Congratulations again, grandfather. Enjoy the walk. Good afternoon.
